record_office_terminology.land_records: CNMI Commonwealth Recorder (deeds, liens, land registrations -- centralized on Saipan); CNMI Department of Public Lands (public land leases and homestead grants)
record_office_terminology.probate: CNMI Superior Court (wills, estates, guardianships, adoptions); Trust Territory High Court handled probate matters before 1978
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: CNMI Superior Court, Clerk of Court (marriage licenses from 1978); Trust Territory district court issued licenses before 1978; earlier marriages documented only in Catholic Church records
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1945 (US military administration began civil registration after the Battle of Saipan; Trust Territory government formalized registration from approximately 1952; CNMI Vital Records Office at Commonwealth Health Center on Saipan is the central repository)

burned_county_authority: CNMI Historic Preservation Office, Saipan -- Saipan and Tinian are classified as burned jurisdictions due to catastrophic WWII destruction (Battles of Saipan and Tinian, 1944) which destroyed virtually all pre-war civil records including Japanese colonial administration files, church records, and land documents. Rota was bypassed in WWII and has better record survival. The Northern Islands have never had significant civil records.
administrative_notes: The Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands (CNMI) is a US territory in the western Pacific Ocean consisting of 14 islands in the Mariana archipelago (all islands north of Guam). The islands were inhabited by the Chamorro people for approximately 4,000 years before Spanish colonization began in 1668 with the Jesuit mission of Padre Diego Luis de San Vitores. The Spanish-Chamorro Wars (1672-1698) and the Reduccion policy (forced relocation of Chamorros to concentrated villages) devastated the indigenous population. Spain administered the Marianas until 1899, when the Northern Marianas were sold to Germany after the Spanish-American War (Guam was ceded to the United States). Germany administered the islands from 1899-1914, introducing copra (coconut) plantations. Japan received the islands as a League of Nations mandate after WWI and transformed them through intensive sugar cultivation, bringing tens of thousands of Japanese, Okinawan, and Korean workers. By the 1930s, Japanese settlers vastly outnumbered the indigenous Chamorro and Carolinian populations. The Battles of Saipan (June-July 1944) and Tinian (July-August 1944) were among the most destructive of the Pacific War, causing massive casualties and the destruction of virtually all civil infrastructure and records. After WWII, the US administered the islands as part of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (1947-1978) under a UN mandate. The CNMI became a US Commonwealth in 1978 (Covenant signed 1975). CNMI residents are US citizens. For genealogical research, the four key record repositories are: (1) Catholic Church sacramental records (the only records spanning the colonial periods); (2) Trust Territory Archives at the University of Hawaii at Manoa (the most comprehensive post-WWII source); (3) CNMI government offices on Saipan (vital records, land records, court records from 1945/1978); and (4) Japanese colonial records at the National Diet Library and JACAR in Tokyo. Chamorro and Carolinian oral traditions, clan genealogies (i familian in Chamorro), and family connections are essential supplements to the limited written record.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Saipan and Tinian are classified as burned jurisdictions due to the devastating Battles of Saipan (June-July 1944) and Tinian (July-August 1944) during WWII, which destroyed virtually all pre-war civil records, Japanese colonial administration files, and most church records. Rota was bypassed during WWII and has relatively better record survival. The Northern Islands municipality has never had significant civil records due to its small, transient population. Overall, pre-WWII genealogical records for the CNMI are extremely limited, with Catholic Church sacramental records being the primary surviving source from the Spanish colonial period.

burned_jurisdictions.records_lost: Virtually all pre-war civil records destroyed, including Japanese colonial administration files, land records, court records, and most Catholic Church records; Japanese military and civilian records deliberately destroyed or lost during combat

All pre-war civil records destroyed, including Japanese colonial Nanyo Kohatsu Kaisha (sugar company) records, administration files, and church records; post-battle US military occupation of most of the island further complicated record preservation

core_repositories.source_name: Trust Territory Archives -- University of Hawaii at Manoa
core_repositories.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa Library, Pacific Collection
core_repositories.description: The single most important repository for post-WWII CNMI records. The Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (TTPI) Archives hold administrative records from 1947-1986 for all Trust Territory districts including the Mariana Islands. Collections include vital statistics, court records, land records, education records, health records, and administrative correspondence. Essential for genealogical research on CNMI families during the Trust Territory period. The Pacific Collection also holds published materials on Micronesian history and culture.

The CNMI's official historic preservation agency, responsible for documenting and preserving the Commonwealth's cultural heritage. Maintains records of historic sites, archaeological surveys, oral history collections, and historical documentation across all four municipalities. Works closely with the National Park Service (American Memorial Park on Saipan) and manages the CNMI's historic site registry. A key contact for researchers seeking historical and genealogical information about CNMI families.

core_repositories.source_name: Northern Mariana Islands Museum of History and Culture
core_repositories.repository: NMI Museum of History and Culture, Garapan, Saipan
core_repositories.description: Museum and archive holding photographs, oral history recordings, artifacts, and historical documents related to the Northern Mariana Islands. Collections cover Chamorro and Carolinian cultural heritage, Spanish colonial period, Japanese mandate era, WWII, and the Trust Territory period. The museum's oral history collection is particularly valuable for genealogical research, as many elder Chamorros and Carolinians recorded family histories and wartime experiences.

core_repositories.source_name: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) -- CNMI Holdings
core_repositories.repository: NARA, College Park, MD / NARA San Bruno, CA
core_repositories.description: NARA holds US military government records for the Mariana Islands (1944-1947), Department of Interior Trust Territory records, and Department of Navy administrative records. Key record groups include: RG 126 (Office of Territories), RG 313 (Naval Operating Forces), and civil affairs records documenting the transition from military to civilian administration. NARA San Bruno holds Pacific regional records. Essential for the WWII and immediate post-war period.

familysearch_collections.description: FamilySearch has limited but growing collections for the CNMI. Available resources include the FamilySearch Wiki page for Northern Mariana Islands genealogy, which provides research guidance and links to available records. FamilySearch's microfilm holdings may include some Catholic Church records from the Marianas mission. The Chamorro and Carolinian diaspora communities (particularly in Guam, Hawaii, and the US mainland) have contributed family trees and records.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch Wiki -- Northern Mariana Islands Genealogy
familysearch_collections.description: Research guide covering CNMI's unique genealogical challenges including the impact of WWII destruction, the multiple colonial transitions, and the importance of Catholic Church records. Provides links to available digital records and guidance on accessing Trust Territory Archives, Japanese colonial records, and CNMI government offices.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Various / CNMI Library / University of Hawaii Pacific Collection
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: The Marianas Variety (established 1972) is the oldest newspaper in the CNMI. The Saipan Tribune is another major publication. Pre-WWII newspapers in the CNMI were Japanese-language publications (Nan'yo Shimbun, etc.) -- surviving issues may be at the National Diet Library in Tokyo. Post-war newspapers provide obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, and community news. The University of Hawaii Pacific Collection holds some CNMI newspaper archives.

military_records.source_name: WWII Pacific Theater Records -- Battles of Saipan and Tinian -- NARA
military_records.description: US military operational records for the Marianas campaign (1944), including the Battles of Saipan and Tinian. Civil affairs records document Chamorro civilian internment at Camp Susupe (Saipan), civilian casualty reports, and the post-battle reconstitution of the civilian population. Repatriation records for Japanese civilians provide valuable demographic data. The American Memorial Park on Saipan (National Park Service) preserves WWII history and may have supplementary records.

military_records.source_name: Japanese Military and Colonial Records -- JACAR / National Diet Library
military_records.repository: Japan Center for Asian Historical Records (JACAR) / National Diet Library, Tokyo
military_records.description: JACAR (Japan Center for Asian Historical Records) has digitized Japanese colonial and military records for the South Seas Mandate (Nan'yo-cho), including the Mariana Islands. Records include colonial administration files, population registers, school records, and military garrison records. The National Diet Library holds published materials, census data, and government reports from the Japanese mandate period (1914-1944). These records are primarily in Japanese and require translation.

land_and_property.source_name: CNMI Land Records -- Commonwealth Recorder / Department of Public Lands
land_and_property.repository: CNMI Commonwealth Recorder, Saipan / CNMI Department of Public Lands
land_and_property.description: The CNMI Commonwealth Recorder maintains land registration records from 1978 onward. The Department of Public Lands administers public land leases and homestead programs. Pre-1978 land records are in the Trust Territory Archives at the University of Hawaii. Land tenure in the CNMI has a complex history: traditional Chamorro matrilineal inheritance, Spanish crown land grants, German colonial land policies, Japanese-era sugar company land seizures, US military land acquisition (especially on Tinian), and Trust Territory homestead programs. Land adjudication records in the Trust Territory court files document traditional land claims and family relationships.

vital_records.source_name: CNMI Vital Records Office -- Commonwealth Health Center
vital_records.repository: CNMI Vital Records Office, Commonwealth Health Center, Saipan
vital_records.description: The central repository for CNMI birth, death, and marriage certificates. Civil registration began under US military administration in 1945 and was formalized by the Trust Territory government from approximately 1952. The Vital Records Office on Saipan serves all four municipalities. Pre-1945 vital events are not documented in civil records and must be reconstructed from Catholic Church sacramental registers. Delayed birth certificates may exist for individuals born before civil registration who later applied for documentation.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Catholic Church Records -- Diocese of Chalan Kanoa / Archdiocese of Agana
church_and_cemetery.repository: Diocese of Chalan Kanoa, Saipan / Archdiocese of Agana, Guam / Capuchin Archives
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic Church sacramental records (baptisms, marriages, burials) are the single most important genealogical source for the CNMI, spanning from the earliest Jesuit mission in 1668 through the present. The Marianas mission was served by Jesuits (1668-1769), Augustinian Recollects (1769-1899), and Capuchin friars (from 1907). The Diocese of Chalan Kanoa was established in 1984 (previously under the Archdiocese of Agana, Guam). Sacramental records may be held at individual parishes, the diocesan archives, the Agana archdiocesan archives, or Capuchin order archives in Europe. Many pre-WWII records were destroyed, but some survive in copies or transcriptions sent to religious order headquarters. The Catholic Church provided the only continuous institutional presence across all colonial administrations.

Cemetery records across the CNMI including Catholic cemeteries on Saipan (Chalan Kanoa Cemetery), Tinian (San Jose Cemetery), and Rota (Songsong Cemetery). The American Memorial Park on Saipan documents WWII casualties. Japanese-era cemeteries and memorials exist on Saipan and Tinian. FindAGrave has limited but growing coverage of CNMI cemeteries. Traditional Chamorro and Carolinian burial practices may not have left written records.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: CNMI Historic Preservation Office -- Research Resources
research_guides_and_societies.description: The CNMI HPO provides research guidance for historical and genealogical inquiries about the Northern Mariana Islands. Maintains oral history collections, site documentation, and historical reference materials. Can direct researchers to appropriate sources for family history research in the CNMI.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Pacific Islands Studies -- University of Hawaii
research_guides_and_societies.repository: University of Hawaii at Manoa, Center for Pacific Islands Studies
research_guides_and_societies.description: Academic center with faculty expertise in Micronesian history and culture. The university's Pacific Collection, Hamilton Library, holds an extensive collection of published and unpublished materials on the Northern Mariana Islands. The Trust Territory Archives are housed here. Researchers can access finding aids, microfilm, and reference materials. The center also produces publications on Pacific Islands history relevant to CNMI genealogical research.

historical_counties_extinct.description: The CNMI has no extinct municipalities. The four current municipalities (Northern Islands, Rota, Saipan, and Tinian) were established with the creation of the CNMI Commonwealth government in 1978. Before 1978, the islands were administered as part of the Mariana Islands District of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (1947-1978). Before the Trust Territory, the islands were under Japanese mandate administration as part of the Nan'yo-cho (South Seas Bureau, 1914-1944), with administrative districts roughly corresponding to individual islands. Under German administration (1899-1914), the islands were part of German New Guinea. Under Spanish administration (1668-1899), the Marianas were governed as a single province from Guam, with the Northern Islands as dependencies. For pre-1978 research, records are organized by colonial administration, not by municipality.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Chamorro and Carolinian Heritage and Cultural Records
native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: CNMI Historic Preservation Office / NMI Museum of History and Culture / University of Guam
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: The Chamorro people are the indigenous inhabitants of the Mariana Islands with a cultural history spanning approximately 4,000 years. Carolinian people settled in the Northern Marianas from the Caroline Islands beginning in the early 1800s. Genealogical information for these communities is found in: oral history recordings at the CNMI HPO and NMI Museum; clan genealogies (i familian in Chamorro) maintained by family elders; Catholic Church records documenting baptism names and godparent relationships; Spanish colonial population records (padrones); and academic studies of Chamorro and Carolinian kinship systems. The University of Guam's Micronesian Area Research Center (MARC) holds extensive materials on Chamorro culture and history. Traditional Chamorro society was matrilineal, with land and clan identity passing through the mother's line -- this is essential context for interpreting genealogical records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Japanese Colonial Immigration and Settler Records -- CNMI
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: National Diet Library, Tokyo / JACAR / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii
immigration_and_naturalization.description: During the Japanese mandate period (1914-1944), tens of thousands of Japanese, Okinawan, and Korean workers immigrated to the Northern Marianas for the sugar industry. The Nan'yo-cho maintained family registers (koseki) for Japanese nationals and some administrative records for indigenous populations. After WWII, all Japanese civilians were repatriated to Japan. Repatriation records at NARA and Japanese government archives document these families. Post-war immigration to the CNMI has included Filipino, Chinese, Korean, and other Asian communities -- CNMI immigration records are held by CNMI government offices. CNMI residents received US citizenship under the 1978 Commonwealth Covenant.
